text:
There was a little girl named Molly who loved collecting butterflies. She would spend her weekends exploring the hedges and underfields in search of rare species. There was one particular kind of butterfly that had eluded her for weeks — the elusive pebble-eyed.

The first autumn had dropped a few, but most were away in Brazil. There was a particular clearing where the ferns swayed gently beneath a crescent moon scattered stars.

When Molly finally found it, it was under a moss-green canopy, with silver crescents mooned on leaves. There was a gentle babbling nearby. It was a small pool of water where the bees hummed happily.

On the edge of the water lay the butterfly. It flicked its delicate wings and fluttered its little brown eyes toward her. Molly chased it for hours until it sat on the leafy branch waving its stubby antennae.

Molly had captured it on her leaf-shaped feathered net. The butterfly was a faded peach, speckled
